did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), a deep learning algorithm, to classify melanoma skin cancer lesions as malignant or benign. The study implemented a CNN architecture trained on a dataset of 10,000 dermoscopy images to achieve diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the implementation and training of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture on a dataset of 10,000 dermoscopy images. The paper includes specific methodology sections (Dataset, CNN Architecture), reports original experimental results (F1 score of 90.87%, sensitivity of 92.46%, specificity of 92.23%), and compares these new findings against prior works in Table 1, rather than summarizing existing literature.

title: Melanoma Skin Cancer Classification based on CNN Deep Learning Algorithms
journal: Malaysian Journal of Fundamental and Applied Sciences
brief_summary: This study presents a deep learning system using a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify melanoma skin cancer lesions as malignant or benign. The model was trained and evaluated on a dataset of 10,000 dermoscopy images, with 9,600 images for training and 1,000 for testing. The proposed CNN architecture consists of five convolutional layers, each followed by a pooling layer, and concludes with a fully connected layer for binary classification. The method achieved an F1 score of 90.87%, a sensitivity of 92.46%, and a specificity of 92.23%. These results demonstrate that the proposed technique excels beyond state-of-the-art methods in terms of diagnostic accuracy, potentially aiding dermatologists in the early detection of melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task. The study distinguishes malignant melanoma from benign lesions using a dataset of 10,000 dermoscopy images.
The negative class in the binary classification task. The dataset contains two classes: malignant and benign. The paper notes difficulty in distinguishing melanoma from a benign mole in early stages.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Malignant vs. Benign melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for binary classification of melanoma skin cancer lesions. The model distinguishes between two discrete classes: malignant and benign. The dataset contains 10,000 images (9,600 for training, 1,000 for testing). The final layer uses a linear transfer function with 2 neurons for the two-class classification task. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (91%), F1 score (90.87%), sensitivity (92.46%), and specificity (92.23%).

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopy images used for binary classification of melanoma skin cancer. The paper does not specify the original source or repository of this dataset, referring to it only as 'Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images'.

use.training: Used to train the proposed CNN architecture (9,600 images).
use.testing: Used for evaluation of the model (1,000 images).

Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Im.notes: The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 91%, which is compared against other works in Table 1. The paper notes that without dermoscopic imaging, dermatologists typically have a 60% success rate in diagnosing melanoma.

architecture_used.pretraining: The abstract states the CNN classifier was 'trained with large amounts of data' and the text mentions 'Images are input into a deep learning model known as a convolutional neural network (CNN) that has already been pre-trained.' However, specific pretraining datasets (e.g., ImageNet) or weights are not explicitly named for the proposed model, only that it was trained on the melanoma dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Images were resized to have 300 pixels on the longest side.

architecture_details.structure: Five convolutional layers, each followed by a single pooling layer.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to have 300 pixels on the longest side
Preprocessing digital photographs affected by noise and lighting effects to enable better feature extraction by the CNN
Data augmentation techniques mentioned in related works (referenced but not explicitly applied in the proposed method's specific pipeline description): rotation, cropping, and resizing

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned in the context of related works (e.g., Nasr-Esfahani et al. [21] and Kwasigroch et al. [27]) as methods to enhance sample numbers or improve classification accuracy. Specific settings for Nasr-Esfahani et al. included cropping at 5% and 10%, and rotation at 0, 90, 180, and 270 degrees. For Kwasigroch et al., transformation methods included rotation, flip, and shift. The proposed method in this paper does not explicitly report applying these augmentations during its own training pipeline, noting only that 'additional augmented data' could potentially improve accuracy.

conclusions: The proposed custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) successfully distinguished between malignant and noncancerous (benign) melanoma lesions.
The method achieved a diagnostic accuracy of 91%, an F1 score of 90.87%, a sensitivity of 92.46%, and a specificity of 92.23%.

The proposed technique excels beyond state-of-the-art methods and previous works (such as those by Nasr-Esfahani et al., Ayan and Unver, Kwasigroch et al., Lopez et al., and Ali and Al-Marzouqi) in terms of diagnostic accuracy.

The system has the potential to aid dermatologists in the early detection of melanoma, particularly given that dermatologists typically have only a 60% success rate in diagnosing melanoma without dermoscopic imaging.
Classification accuracy may be further improved by incorporating additional augmented data into the network.
The study acknowledges that subpar image quality in the dataset naturally reduces the classifier's efficacy.
important_remarks: The dataset source is unspecified ('Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images'), raising concerns about data provenance and potential selection bias.
The paper does not report a validation split; the dataset was divided only into training (9,600 images) and testing (1,000 images), which risks overfitting and optimistic performance estimates.
The study acknowledges that subpar image quality in the dataset naturally reduces the classifier's efficacy, indicating potential robustness issues in real-world clinical settings.
No data augmentation techniques were explicitly applied to the proposed model's training pipeline, despite the paper noting that augmentation could improve accuracy.
The comparison with state-of-the-art methods in Table 1 is limited to older studies (2014-2018) with lower accuracy (78-81.6%), which may not reflect current performance standards.
The CNN architecture uses a linear transfer function for the final classification layer, which is unconventional for binary classification tasks typically requiring sigmoid or softmax activations.
The pretraining details are vague; while the abstract mentions the CNN was 'pre-trained,' the specific pretraining dataset (e.g., ImageNet) and weights are not explicitly named in the methodology.
The study was conducted on a server with a GPU, but hardware specifications for the proposed model's inference are not detailed, limiting reproducibility of computational efficiency claims.
